text: In this video, we will revisit rectal sacral and vagus fascia by laparoscopic or robotic approach.
The robotic instruments are used to dissect the rectal sacral and vagus fascia, with precise movements visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon incises the junction between the proper rectal fascia and the parietal layer of the pelvic fascia, entering the pre-sacral space.
The surgeon further develops the pre-sacral space downward along the Holy Plane, characterized by loose areolar tissues, using a laparoscopic or robotic approach.
The surgeon continues the posterior mobilization of the rectum, advancing dissection until reaching the fourth sacral body.

At the level of the fourth sacral body, a thick, tough, opaque fascia is visible, extending from the periosteum over the vertebral body to the proper fascia of the rectum, approximately three to five centimeters above the anorectal junction. This structure is identified as the recto-sacral fascia.
The surgeon incises the recto-sacral fascia, allowing entry into the retro-rectal space, revealing the anatomical structures within this area.
In the retro-rectal space, the surgeon observes a dense fascia covering the levator ani muscles and the anococcygeal raphe, identified as the Waldeyer's fascia.
Therefore, the recto-sacral fascia and valdius fascia should be the two distinct anatomical structures.
The surgeon performs macroscopic dissection of the recto-sacral and valdius fascia, revealing the distinct anatomical structures within the retro-rectal space.
The surgeon develops the pre-sacral space along the holy plane, continuing the dissection within the retro-rectal space previously entered by incising the recto-sacral fascia.

At the level of the third sacral body, the surgeon encounters a thin membranous fibrous tissue that separates the pre-sacral space into superior and inferior compartments, identified as the recto-sacral fascia.
The surgeon cuts the recto-sacral fascia, allowing entry into the retro-rectal space, revealing the anatomical structures within.
The surgeon observes the dense Valdez fascia at the floor of the retro-rectal space, covering the levator ani muscle and the anococcygeal raphe.
Case number three shows Vardy's fascia was more obvious in patients after concurrent chemoradiation therapy.
The surgeon develops the presacral space downward to the level of the fourth sacral body, revealing the rectal sacral fascia extending from the fourth sacral body to the proper fascia of the rectum.
The surgeon incises the rectal sacral fascia at the floor of the retro-rectal space, revealing the dense Valdias fascia covering the underlying structures.
The video displays the VADAS fascia and rectal sacral fascia, which appear more pronounced in patients following concurrent chemoradiation therapy.
